description:
You are working as a database-reliability engineer.  
  A colleague exported a raw backup inventory as a CSV file located at  
  
      /home/user/backups/all_backups.csv  
  
  The file uses a comma ( , ) as the field separator and contains exactly six
  columns in the following order:

      1. backup_id
      2. db_name
      3. timestamp
      4. size_mb
      5. compressed_size_mb
      6. checksum

  The very first line is a header line with the literal texts shown above.
  Four data lines follow it.  
  Your task is to prepare a concise, tab-separated overview containing only the
  columns that on-call staff actually care about:  

      • backup_id (original column 1)  
      • db_name   (original column 2)  
      • size_mb   (original column 4)

  Requirements for the overview file:

      1. The overview must be written to
         /home/user/backups/backup_overview.tsv  
      2. Columns must be separated by a single U+0009 TAB character.  
      3. Do NOT include the header row in the output.  
      4. Preserve the original row order from the source file.  
      5. No extra blank lines or trailing spaces are allowed.

  After creating the overview, append a two-line log to
  /home/user/backups/backup_overview_creation.log with the exact format below
  (these two lines must be the only content of the file; overwrite any previous
  file if it exists):

      backup_overview.tsv created successfully
      Total lines: X

  where X is the exact number of data lines that ended up in the overview
  (it should be 4 for the provided input).

  Hints (do not copy-paste—think and type the commands yourself):

      • The cut utility is ideal for slicing specific columns.  
      • Converting commas to tabs can be done with tr or by using cut’s
        --output-delimiter option.  
      • Check your result with cat -t to make sure real TABs were used.  

  Only two or three well-thought-out commands are enough to complete everything.
truth:
Initial state (already present before the agent starts):

    Directory:
/home/user/backups

    File:
/home/user/backups/all_backups.csv
    Contents (6 lines, LF line-endings):
backup_id,db_name,timestamp,size_mb,compressed_size_mb,checksum
bkp-20240101-pg,customerdb,2024-01-01T02:05:00Z,10240,2048,a1b2c3d4
bkp-20240102-mysql,ordersdb,2024-01-02T03:10:00Z,5120,1024,b2c3d4e5
bkp-20240103-pg,inventorydb,2024-01-03T01:55:00Z,20480,4096,c3d4e5f6
bkp-20240104-mysql,analyticsdb,2024-01-04T04:20:00Z,102400,20480,d4e5f6g7

    Permissions: the /home/user/backups directory and everything in it are
    owned by user:user and are writable by the user.

  Expected state after correct task completion:

    1. File /home/user/backups/backup_overview.tsv exists with exactly
       the following four lines (TABs between the columns, single LF at the end
       of each line):

bkp-20240101-pg	customerdb	10240
bkp-20240102-mysql	ordersdb	5120
bkp-20240103-pg	inventorydb	20480
bkp-20240104-mysql	analyticsdb	102400

    2. File /home/user/backups/backup_overview_creation.log exists with exactly
       the following two lines (LF line endings, no extra spaces):

backup_overview.tsv created successfully
Total lines: 4

    3. No other files are modified or created.
